items.title: Claude Code 2.1.63 dropped with a new built-in command: /simplify
items.raw_summary:
Claude Code 2.1.63 dropped with a new built-in command: /simplify

It reviews your changed code for three things:

- Reuse opportunities (duplicated logic, extractable patterns)
- Code quality (readability, naming, structure)
- Efficiency (unnecessary complexity, redundant operations)

Then it actually fixes what it finds. Not just suggestions, it edits.

How to use it:
1. Make your code changes as usual
2. Run /simplify
3. It analyzes your diff, finds issues, and applies fixes

In the video I r
items.why_it_matters: Claude Code 2.1.63 ships /simplify — a built-in command that reviews changed code for duplicated logic, readability issues, and unnecessary complexity, then auto-fixes them.
items.action: Run /simplify after your next code change

items.title: 🚨BREAKING: The "Cursor for scientific research" just dropped and it runs entirely inside Claude Cod
items.raw_summary:
🚨BREAKING: The "Cursor for scientific research" just dropped and it runs entirely inside Claude Code.

It's called claude-scientific-skills and it gives Claude 140 ready-to-use scientific skills from a single plugin install.

No API doc hunting. No library configuration. No duct-taped research pipelines.

It's powered by 28+ live scientific databases wired directly into Claude.

→ Describe your research goal in plain English
→ Claude finds the right skill automatically
→ Full pipeline runs: dat
items.why_it_matters: claude-scientific-skills provides 140 ready-to-use scientific skills connected to 28+ live databases, turning Claude Code into a scientific research assistant.
items.action: Install claude-scientific-skills if you do computational research

items.title: 🦔 Jason Calacanis says his company hit $300/day per agent using Claude's API at only 10-20% capacit
items.raw_summary:
🦔 Jason Calacanis says his company hit $300/day per agent using Claude's API at only 10-20% capacity, which scales to around $100,000/year per agent. Chamath Palihapitiya added that he's now asking "what's the token budget for our best devs?" and said AI-assisted developers need to be at least 2x as productive just to justify the cost. He said this is actively happening inside his company or he'll run out of money.

My Take
This was always the obvious trajectory. AI providers subsidized usage t
items.why_it_matters: Jason Calacanis reveals $300/day per AI coding agent at 10-20% capacity, scaling to ~$100K/year — concrete production cost data that challenges ROI assumptions.
items.action: Benchmark your own per-agent costs against this data point

items.title: Is Traditional Software Engineering Dead?
items.raw_summary:
Is Traditional Software Engineering Dead?

“Does this mean that traditional software engineering is dead? Absolutely not. Software engineers—even the ones who are not necessarily tuning or training AI models—these are now among the most leveraged people on earth. Sure, the guys who are training and tuning models are even more leveraged because they’re building the tool set that software engineers are using.

But software engineers still have two massive advantages on you. First, they think in co
items.why_it_matters: Naval Ravikant argues software engineers are now among the most leveraged people on earth thanks to AI tools — a framing that shifts the narrative from replacement to amplification.
items.action: Consider how AI leverage changes your team's hiring and tooling strategy

items.title: 📦 ggerganov/llama.cpp b8198
items.raw_summary: <details open>  ggml: fix ggml_is_contiguous_n for ne == 1 (#20092)  </details>  **macOS/iOS:** - [macOS Apple Silicon (arm64)](https://github.com/ggml-org/llama.cpp/releases/download/b8198/llama-b8198-bin-macos-arm64.tar.gz) - [macOS Intel (x64)](https://github.com/ggml-org/llam
items.why_it_matters: llama.cpp b8198 fixes ggml_is_contiguous_n — a subtle tensor contiguity bug that could cause silent correctness issues in quantized inference.
items.action: Update llama.cpp if running quantized models

items.title: 🚨 Someone just solved the biggest bottleneck in AI agents. And it's a 12MB binary.
items.raw_summary:
🚨 Someone just solved the biggest bottleneck in AI agents. And it's a 12MB binary.

It's called Pinchtab. It gives any AI agent full browser control through a plain HTTP API.

Not locked to a framework. Not tied to an SDK. Any agent, any language, even curl.

No config. No setup. No dependencies. Just a single Go binary.

Here's why every existing solution is broken:

→ OpenClaw's browser? Only works inside OpenClaw
→ Playwright MCP? Framework-locked
→ Browser Use? Coupled to its own stack

Pin
items.why_it_matters: Pinchtab is a single 12MB Go binary that gives any AI agent full browser control via plain HTTP API — framework-agnostic and zero-dependency.
items.action: Test Pinchtab as a lightweight browser automation layer for your agents

items.title: 🚨 BREAKING: A developer on GitHub just built a tool that turns any GitHub repo into an interactive
items.raw_summary:
🚨 BREAKING: A developer on GitHub just built a tool that turns any GitHub repo into an interactive knowledge graph and open sourced it for free.

It's called GitNexus. Think of it as a visual X-ray of your codebase but with an AI agent you can actually talk to.

No server. No subscription. No enterprise sales call.

Here's what it does inside your browser:

→ Parses your entire GitHub repo or ZIP file in seconds
→ Builds a live interactive knowledge graph with D3.js
→ Maps every function, class
items.why_it_matters: GitNexus turns any GitHub repo into an interactive knowledge graph with an AI agent — useful for onboarding onto unfamiliar codebases.
items.action: Try GitNexus on a complex repo you need to understand quickly
